title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: [37]The explosive commercial rise of direct-to-consumer genetic testing services represents a profound privacy nightmare carefully disguised as an exciting voyage of personal self-discovery and ancestral connection.[/37] [38]When you voluntarily hand over your most intimate biological data to a private corporation in exchange for an ancestry percentage report, you are not merely compromising your own privacy, but simultaneously and irrevocably surrendering the genetic information of every biological relative who never provided their informed consent to any such commercial arrangement.[/38] [39]These rapidly growing commercial DNA databases constitute an extraordinary goldmine of sensitive and commercially exploitable information for insurance companies, pharmaceutical corporations, and law enforcement agencies, and no amount of claimed 'anonymization' can truly or permanently protect the uniquely individual biological blueprint of any human being from determined future exploitation.[/39] We are recklessly and voluntarily surrendering our most intimate biological secrets for the price of a consumer novelty report. The consequences for future generations could prove severe and far-reaching.
texts.B: Genetic testing technology has genuinely and dramatically transformed our collective understanding of human health, heritable disease risk, and biological ancestry, empowering growing numbers of individuals to take a meaningfully proactive and informed role in managing their own long-term personal wellbeing. By identifying specific genetic predispositions to serious medical conditions early and accurately, informed users can make targeted lifestyle adjustments and seek appropriate preventative medical guidance—changes that demonstrably save lives and meaningfully reduce long-term burdens on healthcare systems. [37]The frequently expressed privacy concerns surrounding these services are largely overstated; the vast majority of reputable companies maintain strict and independently audited data protection protocols, sharing information with researchers only when users provide clear and fully informed consent.[/37] To excessively restrict or block this genuinely transformative technology would be to deny millions of people access to potentially life-saving preventative health information and the profound personal experience of connecting more deeply and accurately with their ancestral heritage.
texts.C: [40]I agree that the concrete health insights and detailed ancestral information provided by consumer genetic tests represent a significant and genuine personal benefit for many hundreds of thousands of users worldwide.[/40] However, we must urgently address the dangerous tendency toward 'biological determinism' that these commercially marketed tests can inadvertently promote among scientifically non-specialist users. A statistically elevated genetic predisposition to a particular disease is most emphatically not a personal destiny or an inevitable medical sentence, yet many users experience serious unnecessary health anxiety or make radical medical decisions based on fundamentally misunderstood probabilistic data. I share the critical view that the near-total absence of meaningful regulation governing the secondary commercial use of collected genetic data is a major unresolved systemic vulnerability. [38]We need a comprehensive and legally binding 'Genetic Bill of Rights' that unambiguously ensures our most sensitive biological data cannot be weaponized against us by employers or insurers, regardless of what the fine print on a commercial website purports to permit.[/38]
texts.D: The most philosophically troubling aspect of the current consumer DNA testing phenomenon is the subtle but profound commodification of personal and collective identity itself. Identity is fundamentally a social, historical, and cultural construction shaped by lived experience, community belonging, and personal narrative—it is not reducible to a numerical percentage figure generated by a commercial laboratory. By reductively describing ourselves as '40% Scandinavian' or '10% West African,' we risk unconsciously reinscribing and commercially reinforcing old racial and ethnic categories that have no credible basis in contemporary population genetics. [39]While I share the serious concern about law enforcement agencies obtaining unrestricted access to these growing commercial databases, I believe the deeper harm lies in the way these products distract us from engaging honestly and courageously with the complex lived reality of our communities and actual relationships.[/39] [40]We gaze eagerly backward into a constructed genetic past as a convenient means of avoiding the genuine complexities of the present.[/40]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the severity of the privacy risks associated with DNA testing?
questions.explanation: Expert B says privacy concerns are 'largely overblown.' Expert A calls it a 'privacy nightmare' and says 'no amount of anonymization' can protect the data.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's opinion about the potential for third-party organizations (like insurers) to exploit genetic data?
questions.explanation: Expert A mentions these databases are a 'goldmine for insurance companies.' Expert C also warns that DNA could be 'used against us by employers or insurers.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to D regarding the misuse of genetic data by the authorities?
questions.explanation: Expert D shares the concern about 'law enforcement access to these databases.' Expert A also explicitly lists 'law enforcement' as one of the groups for whom these databases are a goldmine.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on the primary value of the health information these tests provide?
questions.explanation: Expert C believes the health insights are a 'significant benefit.' Expert D, however, argues that the focus on such data is a 'distraction' from 'lived reality' and reinforces problematic categories.
